Benzoyl Peroxide.indications: Mild to moderate acne vulgaris; reduces P. acnes bacteria and unclogs pores.
Benzoyl Peroxide.contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to benzoyl peroxide; avoid on broken or sunburned skin.
Benzoyl Peroxide.side_effects: Dryness, peeling, redness, mild stinging; can bleach hair and fabric.
Benzoyl Peroxide.pregnancy_warning: Generally considered low-risk topically; discuss with a doctor before use in pregnancy.
Benzoyl Peroxide.evidence_source: FDA OTC monograph; AAD acne guidelines.

Adapalene.indications: Comedonal and mild inflammatory acne; normalizes follicular keratinization.
Adapalene.contraindications: Eczema-prone or sunburned skin; avoid with other strong exfoliants concurrently.
Adapalene.side_effects: Dryness, peeling, photosensitivity, initial purging breakout.
Adapalene.pregnancy_warning: Topical retinoids are generally avoided in pregnancy; consult a physician.
Adapalene.evidence_source: FDA-approved OTC switch (2016); AAD acne guidelines.

Tretinoin.indications: Acne vulgaris and photoaging; increases skin cell turnover.
Tretinoin.contraindications: Pregnancy, eczema, sunburned or broken skin.
Tretinoin.pregnancy_warning: Contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic risk associated with retinoids).

Azelaic Acid.indications: Acne and rosacea; reduces inflammation and bacterial growth.
Azelaic Acid.side_effects: Mild burning, itching, or stinging on application.
Azelaic Acid.pregnancy_warning: Considered one of the safer topical options in pregnancy, but confirm with a doctor.

Isotretinoin.contraindications: Pregnancy (absolute contraindication), liver disease, vitamin A supplementation.
Isotretinoin.side_effects: Severe dryness, photosensitivity, elevated lipids/liver enzymes, mood changes, birth defects if pregnant.
Isotretinoin.pregnancy_warning: Absolutely contraindicated — requires confirmed negative pregnancy tests and contraception (iPLEDGE-type programs).
Isotretinoin.evidence_source: FDA label; AAD severe acne guidelines.

Hydrocortisone.indications: Mild eczema, dermatitis, insect bites, and general itchy rashes.
Hydrocortisone.contraindications: Active untreated skin infection (fungal, bacterial, viral) at the site.
Hydrocortisone.side_effects: Skin thinning with prolonged use, mild burning on application.
Hydrocortisone.pregnancy_warning: Low-potency topical steroids are generally considered low-risk; confirm with a doctor for prolonged use.
Hydrocortisone.age_limit: 2+ for OTC strength; consult a doctor for infants.
Hydrocortisone.evidence_source: FDA OTC monograph; AAD eczema guidelines.

Mometasone.indications: Moderate eczema, psoriasis plaques, and inflammatory dermatoses.
Mometasone.contraindications: Untreated skin infections; long-term use on the face, groin, or armpits.
Mometasone.side_effects: Skin thinning, stretch marks, telangiectasia with prolonged use.
Mometasone.pregnancy_warning: Use the lowest effective amount; discuss with a physician.
Mometasone.evidence_source: AAD atopic dermatitis guidelines; FDA label.

Tacrolimus.indications: Moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis, especially on sensitive areas like the face.
Tacrolimus.contraindications: Immunosuppression-related skin infections; not for use under occlusion.
Tacrolimus.side_effects: Burning or stinging on initial application, rare lymphoma signal (boxed warning).
Tacrolimus.pregnancy_warning: Limited data; use only if benefits outweigh risks, under medical supervision.
Tacrolimus.evidence_source: FDA boxed-warning label; AAD atopic dermatitis guidelines.

Pimecrolimus.indications: Mild-to-moderate atopic dermatitis on sensitive skin areas.
Pimecrolimus.contraindications: Active skin infections at treatment site.
Pimecrolimus.side_effects: Application-site burning, headache (uncommon), rare lymphoma signal (boxed warning).
Pimecrolimus.pregnancy_warning: Limited data; use under medical supervision only.
Pimecrolimus.evidence_source: FDA boxed-warning label; AAD atopic dermatitis guidelines.

Prednisone.indications: Severe inflammatory or autoimmune skin disease (e.g., vasculitis) under specialist care.
Prednisone.contraindications: Systemic fungal infection, uncontrolled diabetes (relative), live vaccines during high-dose therapy.
Prednisone.side_effects: Weight gain, mood changes, elevated blood sugar, bone thinning with long-term use.
Prednisone.pregnancy_warning: Used only when clearly needed and under specialist supervision.
Prednisone.age_limit: All ages as prescribed and dosed by a physician.

Cephalexin.indications: Bacterial cellulitis, impetigo, and other skin/soft-tissue infections.
Cephalexin.contraindications: Known cephalosporin allergy; caution with severe penicillin allergy.
Cephalexin.side_effects: GI upset, diarrhea, allergic rash (rare), antibiotic-associated colitis (rare).
Cephalexin.pregnancy_warning: Generally considered compatible with pregnancy; confirm dose with an obstetric provider.
Cephalexin.age_limit: All ages, dosed by weight in children, as prescribed.
Cephalexin.evidence_source: IDSA skin & soft tissue infection guidelines.

Amoxicillin-Clavulanate.indications: Bacterial cellulitis and mixed skin infections, especially with resistant organisms.
Amoxicillin-Clavulanate.contraindications: Penicillin allergy, history of amoxicillin-associated cholestatic jaundice.
Amoxicillin-Clavulanate.side_effects: Diarrhea, nausea, rash, rare liver enzyme elevation.
Amoxicillin-Clavulanate.pregnancy_warning: Generally considered compatible with pregnancy; confirm with a provider.
Amoxicillin-Clavulanate.age_limit: All ages, dosed by weight in children.
Amoxicillin-Clavulanate.evidence_source: IDSA skin & soft tissue infection guidelines.

Clindamycin.indications: Bacterial cellulitis, acne (topical form), and infections in penicillin-allergic patients.
Clindamycin.side_effects: Diarrhea (including risk of C. diff colitis), GI upset, rash.
Clindamycin.pregnancy_warning: Generally considered usable in pregnancy when indicated; confirm with a provider.
Clindamycin.evidence_source: IDSA skin & soft tissue infection guidelines.

Doxycycline.indications: Moderate inflammatory acne and rosacea, used for its anti-inflammatory effect.
Doxycycline.side_effects: Photosensitivity, GI upset, esophageal irritation if taken without water.
Doxycycline.pregnancy_warning: Contraindicated — tetracyclines can affect fetal bone/teeth development.
Doxycycline.age_limit: 8+ only, due to tooth discoloration risk in younger children.

Minocycline.indications: Moderate-to-severe inflammatory acne unresponsive to topical therapy.
Minocycline.side_effects: Dizziness, photosensitivity, rare skin/mucosal pigmentation with long-term use.
Minocycline.pregnancy_warning: Contraindicated — tetracyclines affect fetal bone/teeth development.

Mupirocin.indications: Localized impetigo and minor bacterial skin infections.
Mupirocin.contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to mupirocin or polyethylene glycol.
Mupirocin.pregnancy_warning: Topical use generally considered low systemic risk; confirm with a provider.

Retapamulin.indications: Localized impetigo caused by susceptible bacteria.
Retapamulin.pregnancy_warning: Limited data; topical use considered low systemic exposure — confirm with a provider.

Clotrimazole.indications: Athlete's foot, ringworm, and other superficial dermatophyte/yeast infections.
Clotrimazole.side_effects: Mild burning, redness, or irritation at application site.
Clotrimazole.pregnancy_warning: Topical use generally considered low-risk; confirm with a doctor.

Terbinafine.indications: Athlete's foot, ringworm, and fungal nail infections.
Terbinafine.side_effects: Topical: irritation. Oral: GI upset, rare liver enzyme elevation, taste disturbance.
Terbinafine.pregnancy_warning: Oral terbinafine generally avoided in pregnancy unless clearly necessary; topical form lower risk.
Terbinafine.age_limit: 12+ for topical OTC; oral form per clinician guidance.
Terbinafine.evidence_source: FDA label; AAD fungal infection guidelines.

Miconazole.indications: Athlete's foot, ringworm, and other dermatophyte infections.
Miconazole.pregnancy_warning: Topical use generally considered low-risk; confirm with a doctor.

Ketoconazole.indications: Ringworm, athlete's foot, and seborrheic-type fungal skin conditions.
Ketoconazole.contraindications: Known hypersensitivity; oral form has significant liver-toxicity and drug-interaction risk (largely phased out orally).
Ketoconazole.side_effects: Topical: irritation, dryness. Oral form (rarely used): liver toxicity risk.
Ketoconazole.pregnancy_warning: Topical use considered lower risk; confirm with a doctor, especially for oral form.
Ketoconazole.evidence_source: FDA label; AAD fungal infection guidelines.

Fluconazole.indications: Fungal nail infections and systemic or resistant fungal skin infections.
Fluconazole.contraindications: Severe liver disease, concurrent use with certain QT-prolonging drugs.
Fluconazole.side_effects: Headache, GI upset, rare liver enzyme elevation, QT prolongation risk.
Fluconazole.pregnancy_warning: High-dose use linked to birth defects; use only if clearly necessary and under medical supervision.
Fluconazole.age_limit: All ages as prescribed and dosed by a physician.
Fluconazole.evidence_source: FDA label; AAD fungal nail infection guidelines.

Itraconazole.indications: Fungal nail infections and resistant dermatophyte infections.
Itraconazole.contraindications: Heart failure history, concurrent use with many interacting drugs.
Itraconazole.side_effects: GI upset, liver enzyme elevation, rare heart failure exacerbation.
Itraconazole.pregnancy_warning: Avoided in pregnancy except in life-threatening infections; consult a physician.
Itraconazole.age_limit: All ages as prescribed by a physician.
Itraconazole.evidence_source: FDA label; AAD fungal nail infection guidelines.

Ciclopirox.pregnancy_warning: Topical use generally considered low systemic risk; confirm with a doctor.
Ciclopirox.evidence_source: FDA label; AAD nail fungus guidelines.

Acyclovir.indications: Herpes simplex, chickenpox, and shingles, especially started early.
Acyclovir.side_effects: Headache, nausea, rare kidney function changes with IV/high oral doses.
Acyclovir.pregnancy_warning: Generally considered usable in pregnancy when clearly indicated; confirm with a provider.
Acyclovir.age_limit: All ages, dosed by weight in children, as prescribed.

Valacyclovir.indications: Herpes simplex, shingles; preferred for less frequent dosing.
Valacyclovir.contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to valacyclovir/acyclovir; caution in advanced HIV or transplant patients (rare TTP/HUS risk).
Valacyclovir.pregnancy_warning: Generally considered usable in pregnancy when clearly indicated; confirm with a provider.

Famciclovir.indications: Herpes simplex and shingles, particularly recurrent episodes.
Famciclovir.contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to famciclovir or penciclovir.
Famciclovir.pregnancy_warning: Limited pregnancy data; use only if clearly needed under medical supervision.
Famciclovir.age_limit: 18+ typically; pediatric use per clinician guidance.

Albendazole.indications: Cutaneous larva migrans and other parasitic infections.
Albendazole.contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to benzimidazole antiparasitics; caution in liver disease.
Albendazole.side_effects: GI upset, headache, rare liver enzyme elevation.
Albendazole.pregnancy_warning: Avoided in the first trimester unless clearly necessary; consult a physician.
Albendazole.age_limit: All ages as prescribed and dosed by a physician.

Ivermectin.indications: Cutaneous larva migrans, scabies, and other parasitic skin infections.
Ivermectin.side_effects: Dizziness, nausea, rare neurological effects at high doses.
Ivermectin.pregnancy_warning: Limited safety data in pregnancy; use only if clearly needed under medical supervision.
Ivermectin.age_limit: Typically 15 kg body weight and above, as prescribed.

Thiabendazole.indications: Localized cutaneous larva migrans lesions (topical cream).
Thiabendazole.side_effects: Local irritation (topical); GI upset and dizziness (oral form, rarely used).
Thiabendazole.pregnancy_warning: Limited data; use only under medical supervision.
Thiabendazole.age_limit: All ages as directed by a physician.

Cetirizine.indications: Itching from eczema, hives, allergic rashes, and chickenpox.
Cetirizine.contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to cetirizine or hydroxyzine.
Cetirizine.pregnancy_warning: Generally considered low-risk; confirm with a doctor, especially in the first trimester.
Cetirizine.evidence_source: FDA OTC label; AAD eczema/hives guidelines.

Loratadine.indications: Itching from hives, allergic rashes, and atopic dermatitis.
Loratadine.pregnancy_warning: Generally considered low-risk; confirm with a doctor.
Loratadine.evidence_source: FDA OTC label; AAD hives guidelines.

Fexofenadine.indications: Chronic hives (urticaria) and other allergic itching.
Fexofenadine.pregnancy_warning: Generally considered low-risk; confirm with a doctor.
Fexofenadine.evidence_source: FDA OTC label; AAD urticaria guidelines.

Minoxidil.indications: Androgenetic alopecia and other patterned hair thinning.
Minoxidil.contraindications: Scalp irritation/sunburn at application site; known hypersensitivity.
Minoxidil.side_effects: Scalp irritation, unwanted facial hair growth if it spreads, initial shedding phase.
Minoxidil.pregnancy_warning: Topical use generally discouraged in pregnancy; confirm with a doctor.
Minoxidil.evidence_source: FDA OTC monograph; AAD hair loss guidelines.

Salicylic Acid.indications: HPV-related skin warts; helps soften and remove thickened skin.
Salicylic Acid.contraindications: Diabetes with poor circulation, open wounds, facial/genital skin.
Salicylic Acid.side_effects: Local irritation, peeling, rare chemical burn with overuse.
Salicylic Acid.pregnancy_warning: Topical low-concentration use generally considered low-risk; confirm with a doctor.

Imiquimod.indications: Genital/external HPV warts; stimulates local immune response against the virus.
Imiquimod.contraindications: Active inflammatory skin conditions at the application site.
Imiquimod.side_effects: Local redness, irritation, flu-like symptoms in some users.
Imiquimod.pregnancy_warning: Limited safety data; use only if clearly indicated under medical supervision.
Imiquimod.evidence_source: FDA label; CDC STI treatment guidelines.

Paracetamol.indications: Fever and mild pain associated with chickenpox, shingles, or general illness.
Paracetamol.contraindications: Severe liver disease; avoid exceeding recommended daily dose.
Paracetamol.side_effects: Rare at recommended doses; liver toxicity with overdose.
Paracetamol.pregnancy_warning: Generally considered the preferred OTC pain/fever option in pregnancy; confirm dose with a doctor.
Paracetamol.age_limit: All ages, dosed by weight in children.

Ibuprofen.indications: Pain management for shingles and other inflammatory skin conditions.
Ibuprofen.contraindications: Active GI ulcer/bleeding, severe kidney disease, third-trimester pregnancy.
Ibuprofen.side_effects: GI upset, rare kidney effects, increased bleeding risk.
Ibuprofen.pregnancy_warning: Avoided in the third trimester; consult a doctor earlier in pregnancy too.
Ibuprofen.age_limit: 6+ months, dosed by weight in children.

no_medication_notes.Benign: Benign lesions usually need no medication — monitor for changes and protect with sunscreen.
no_medication_notes.Heathy: No medication needed — keep up a normal skincare and sun-protection routine.
no_medication_notes.Malignant: Malignant lesions require oncology-directed treatment (surgery, immunotherapy, radiation) — not OTC or topical medicines. See a doctor immediately.
no_medication_notes.Melanoma Skin Cancer Nevi And Moles: Melanoma requires oncology-directed treatment (surgical excision, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, or radiation) — not OTC medicines. See a doctor immediately.
